title: When Healing Spaces Replicate Power Structures: The Body’s Silent Protest
dek: Many healing or movement spaces intended to offer sanctuary can instead trigger tension and unease, as the body's nervous system responds to unspoken hierarchies and power dynamics. What was meant to be liberation sometimes becomes a performance constrained by rules, causing physiological stress signals like jaw clenching and shallow breathing. This article explores how the body remembers these power structures, even when the mind seeks freedom, emphasizing the complexity of somatic experience in healing contexts.
bodyMarkdown:
You come into the room expecting a sanctuary - a place where your body might finally unclench, where the thrum of your nervous system can slow just a fraction. Instead, the air feels thick, not with incense or the sweet herbaceousness of dried lavender, but with an unspoken hierarchy that settles like a weight across your collarbones. The teacher’s voice, once a balm, now presses like sandpaper against the inside of your skull. Your jaw tightens - masseter and temporalis muscles clenched so hard you can almost taste the iron of your own blood. The breath you draw is shallow, pulling only into the upper chest, that tight box of ribs around your heart, as if your diaphragm has forgotten how to dip low and full.


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:42.967Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

This is not the freedom you sought. The movement that promised liberation, that spoke in the language of the body’s wisdom, has folded itself into another set of rules, another chain of command. The somatic map you trusted - where your psoas might unwind, where your trapezius might soften - now signals danger. The levator scapulae, those little muscles that cradle your neck, knot in silent protest. You feel the slick sheen of sweat at the base of your skull, the subtle sting of frustration rising like acid in your throat. You are alert, yes, but not free. The space that once invited your fury to speak now demands silence, or worse, polite acquiescence.

This is not pathology; this is biology. The body remembers what the mind might deny. The tightening of the jaw is not just tension; it is a message, a flare from the limbic system that something in this “healing” environment is replicating the very power structures you hoped to escape. The shallow breath is not mere habit - it is a signal from the autonomic nervous system, a call to arms or to freeze. The body, in its brutal honesty, knows when it’s being co-opted, when liberation has become performance, when the sacred becomes a stage for hierarchy.

Teachers like Karla McLaren remind us that anger is a messenger, a guide, not a failure or a flaw. Here, in this moment, your body is telling you that the movement has shifted from medicine to method, from healing to hierarchy. The muscles don’t lie. Neither does the breath. You are caught in the tension between wanting to belong and needing to breathe freely. And that tension, that tight coil in your body, is where the real work begins.

## The Anatomy of Compliance

You walk into a brightly lit room, the walls painted in hopeful pastels, a space designed to feel open, expansive - liberating, even. But as you settle into your chair, the subtle tightness begins to thread through your trapezius and levator scapulae, the muscles along your neck and shoulders knotting into quiet submission. The air tastes faintly of antiseptic and dried sage, a scent meant to cleanse but instead settling like a thin film over your lungs. This is no accident. The body, always the first to know, catches the dissonance between the promise of freedom and the unspoken rules that color the room - rules that don't need to be shouted to be obeyed.

Resmaa Menakem, in his work on somatic abolitionism, reminds us that trauma does not live solely in the mind’s shadowy corridors; it is etched into the fascia, the connective tissue that stitches us together. It’s in the tightening of the masseter, the jaw clenched so tightly it threatens to fracture, in the shallow rise and fall of the diaphragm constricted beneath ribs that are braced against the vulnerability of the moment. Trauma is a living archive, a biology of compliance that whispers, “stay small, stay quiet, stay safe.”

In spaces that claim to be liberating, this archive often goes unacknowledged. The language might be radical, the intentions earnest, but subtle coercion settles like a mist. The unspoken expectation to smile more softly, to nod in agreement, to soften your tone, to minimize your body’s natural urgency - that is the anatomy of compliance. It is not through overt violence but through these soft violences that the body learns to compress itself, to shrink down like a hedgehog bracing against imagined threats. The psoas, that deep core muscle often called the body’s fight or flight engine, tightens reflexively, a silent sentinel signaling danger even where the eyes see sanctuary.

This is not pathology; this is biology. Your body remembers the patterns of submission drilled into you by generations of survival - how to fold your ribs inwards to hide your heartbeat, how to turn the gaze away just enough to signal “I am not a threat.” These patterns are inscribed beneath the skin, waiting for your awareness to meet them without judgment. To honor the body’s wisdom is to recognize that liberation is not a concept to be proclaimed but a somatic state to be cultivated. It is in the soft release of the diaphragm, the unclenching of the masseter, the slow unwinding of the psoas that true freedom begins - not in the words spoken aloud but in the shoulders unburdened at 3 a.m., when the world is quiet and your body finally dares to breathe.

## The Herbal Support for Disillusionment

You’re sitting in the quiet aftermath, the way your diaphragm tightens just beneath the ribs, a slow constriction that doesn’t quite let you breathe fully. The realization has settled like a stone in your gut: the guru you once trusted, the community you poured your heart into - they were never the sanctuary you imagined. This is not a moral failing, not a crack in your character. It’s the body’s signal, the nervous system’s cry for recalibration. Here is where herbs like Wood Betony and Motherwort step in - not as magic bullets, but as precise, earthy allies tuned to the language of your muscles, your fascia, your nervous tissue.

Wood Betony (Stachys officinalis) is a bitter, mildly astringent herb that grows in the dappled shade of ancient woods, where the air carries the scent of damp earth and moss. It has been called “the herb of the mind” by herbalists like Matthew Wood, and for good reason. When your masseter muscles clench, your jaw tight as if to hold back a scream, Wood Betony gently invites those fibers to soften. It supports the vagus nerve, the wandering messenger that winds through your neck and chest, coaxing it back toward balance. It’s not sedation; it’s a steadying presence, like the quiet voice of Thich Nhat Hanh reminding you to return to your breath amidst the storm of disillusionment.

Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca) is another ally, often overlooked but fiercely loyal to the heart’s nervous system. Its bitter-green leaves and tiny pink flowers hold centuries of use in traditional Chinese and European medicine for nervous agitation and cardiac distress - not the kind that lands you in a hospital, but the kind that tightens the levator scapulae and trapezius as you wrestle with betrayal and loss. Motherwort’s bitters awaken the parasympathetic nervous system, encouraging the diaphragm to release its grip and the heart to slow from the frenetic pace of fear. It’s Robert Augustus Masters’ somatic wisdom embodied in leaf form: the body remembers, but it can also forgive.

When you sip a tincture of Wood Betony or Motherwort, the taste is not sweet or syrupy; it’s sharp, a little biting, grounding you in the present moment. This is not about denial or escapism. It’s about meeting the body’s truth with respect. Gabor Maté teaches us that trauma isn’t what happens to you - it’s what happens inside you afterward. These herbs are tools to transform that inner landscape, to soothe the tight bands of fascia that wrap around your ribs like barbed wire. They support the nervous system’s recalibration, the subtle rewiring that lets you move from contraction to expansion, from betrayal to a clearer, more embodied knowing.

Find Motherwort [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20) and Wood Betony [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20). Use them with reverence, with patience, and with the understanding that your body holds the map to your healing - your jaw remembers, and it’s time to listen.

## The Rebellion of the Psoas

Just beneath your navel, tucked deep inside the belly’s shadowy hollows, lies the psoas - a muscle so intimate to your survival it’s sometimes called the “muscle of the soul.” It is the keeper of your untold stories, the silent witness to every time you swallowed your fury, every moment you bottled up a no into a quiet nod or a forced smile. This deep core muscle doesn’t just hold your body upright; it holds the tension of your unspoken dissent like a clenched fist pressed against the spine of your day-to-day life.

The psoas stretches from the lumbar vertebrae down to the femur, threading through the diaphragm and cradling the kidneys. It’s a physical and emotional fulcrum, the hinge between fight, flight, and freeze. When you learned early on that your anger was a kind of rebellion too dangerous to express, the psoas took on the burden of your silence. The muscle tightens, shortens, and stiffens, locking your protest inside your pelvis, your belly, your very breath. This is not a metaphor but biology. Bessel van der Kolk teaches us that “the body keeps the score,” and nowhere is this more palpably true than in the hidden fortress of your psoas. It records your history without words - your compliance, your fear, your fury folded into itself like a secret note tucked beneath your ribs.

In spiritual or movement hierarchies - those systems where you’ve been taught to rank your feelings, to prioritize calm over storm, stillness over rage - the psoas often rebels quietly. The body knows these hierarchies as rules etched in fascia and muscle, where anger is the unruly guest at the table of mindfulness or grace. Your psoas contracts against the imposed silence, a rebellion not loud or visible but felt as a dull ache in the low back, a hollow tightness in the belly that refuses to release. It is your body’s way of saying, “I remember. I refuse to forget.”

This tension can show up in the way you move - or don’t move - after a long day. Maybe your pelvis feels locked, your gait stiff, as if you’re walking through water made of old grievances. You might notice a sharp catch in the diaphragm, the subtle hitch in the breath when you try to relax. The psoas’s rebellion is the residue of all those times your anger was deemed ugly or dangerous, a rebellion held in the twisting, unyielding coil of muscle that connects your spine to your legs.

Understanding this is not about pathologizing your body’s response but honoring it as an act of resistance. Your psoas is not betraying you; it’s protecting you, carrying the memory of your rebellion so you don’t have to carry it alone. When you invite herbs like [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20) or [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20) to soften this muscle’s grip, or when you engage in somatic movements guided by teachers like Resmaa Menakem who honor the body’s wisdom, you’re not erasing the rebellion - you’re coaxing it into a dialogue. The psoas remembers your fury. It waits. It listens. And when you are ready, it will loosen its hold and let your body speak - finally - its raw, unvarnished truth.

## An Embodied Practice for Reclaiming Your Center

You know the weight of compliance like a slow, unyielding pressure pressing down through your chest, settling into the diaphragm and squeezing the throat. It’s a tension that doesn’t just live in your mind - it’s lodged right in the body’s core, where breath should flow but instead feels caught, clipped like a bird whose wings have been clipped. The diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs, tightens and flattens under the strain of holding back, of swallowing your fury before it can rise. Meanwhile, the masseter - the powerful jaw muscle you use when you grit your teeth - clenches in quiet defiance or muted surrender, a clenched gatekeeper of words unspoken and feelings denied.

This is where your practice begins: in the deep, tactile awareness of these two muscle groups. Find a quiet moment, preferably in the early evening when your body is tired but the mind is still alert - say, around 7 pm, when the air smells like rain on warm earth and the light has softened to a dull, forgiving amber. Sit comfortably, unbolt your shoulders from their habitual shrug, and place one hand gently on the soft spot just under your sternum, where the diaphragm lives.

Begin by drawing your attention to the breath, not the shallow, quick inhales that come with anxiety or compliance, but the slow, deliberate kind that expands the belly and ribs. As you inhale, feel the diaphragm descend, a soft expansion pressing your hand outward. On the exhale, allow the diaphragm to release downward and upward in a wave, like the tide receding from a shore. Notice how this movement feels - can you detect the subtle loosening or the stubborn resistance? This is not a battle; it’s a conversation. Let your breath invite the diaphragm to soften, to reclaim its natural rhythm.

Now, bring your fingers to your jaw. Trace the edges of the masseter, those thick bands of muscle running from your cheekbones to your chin. When was the last time you unclenched this muscle with intention? Gently open your mouth just enough to separate the teeth by the width of a pencil, releasing the grip that hides so much fury. As you breathe out, let your jaw soften, drop a little, and feel the fascia around it give way. If a sigh wants to rise from deep within your belly, welcome it - this is the body’s language for “I’m here, I feel, I resist.”

Repeat this cycle: deep diaphragmatic breath paired with jaw unclenching, over and over, for five to ten minutes. If your mind wanders, bring it back not with judgment but curiosity. Where does resistance still live? Perhaps the levator scapulae - those little muscles at the back of your neck - are still braced, or the trapezius holding your shoulders tight. Soften them too, as you breathe and unclench.

This practice is a reclaiming, a bodily “no” spoken softly but firmly. It’s not about controlling the anger but becoming intimate with its roots in your flesh. As Thich Nhat Hanh taught, the body knows the way home when you listen. Your diaphragm remembers how to flow freely. Your masseter recalls the power of release. This is not pathology, this is biology - your biology saying, “I am here. I am unbound.”

## The Anger of Betrayal

You know the way your throat tightens, the masseter muscle clenches as if bracing for a blow, when someone you trusted becomes the source of your deepest wound. Betrayal’s anger is not the same as the quick flare of irritation or the simmering frustration of unmet expectations; it is a slow, corrosive fire that eats into the soft tissues where your safety once lived. It lodges in the fascia around your sternum, presses down on the diaphragm, and makes breathing feel like a betrayal itself. This is not a moral failure, not a sign you’ve lost your way  -  this is the body’s primal alarm system sounding off. Your biology is screaming that the container you thought was safe has cracked, and the anger that rises is the body’s desperate attempt to seal the breach.

When a safe space becomes unsafe, the betrayal is visceral. It’s not just an intellectual disappointment; it’s a somatic shock. The psoas, that deep core muscle connecting your spine to your legs, tightens reflexively, preparing you to flee or fight, even when your mind is still trying to rationalize the harm. You might wake at 3 a.m., the air thick with the metallic scent of your own sweat, your heart hammering against your ribs. This is the anger of betrayal doing its work. It is a vital messenger, a fierce protector that refuses to let you pretend everything is fine. It demands that you acknowledge the rupture, that you feel the jagged edges of the broken trust.

To treat this anger as a failure of enlightenment is to misunderstand the body entirely. The body does not care about spiritual bypassing or the polite fictions we tell ourselves to keep the peace. The body knows what it knows. When you try to suppress this anger, to smooth it over with platitudes or forced forgiveness, you are only driving the tension deeper into your muscles. The levator scapulae knot up, the trapezius hardens into stone, and the anger becomes a trapped, toxic energy that poisons the very well it was meant to protect.

Instead, you must learn to sit with this anger, to let it burn without consuming you. You must listen to its specific flavor  -  the sharp, metallic taste of injustice, the heavy, suffocating weight of grief. You must honor the way your body is trying to keep you safe, even when the safety it seeks is no longer available. This is the work of true healing: not to transcend the anger, but to descend into it, to let it guide you back to your own sovereign center. The anger of betrayal is not a flaw to be fixed; it is a fire to be tended, a fierce, unapologetic force that insists on your right to be whole, to be safe, and to be heard.

## FAQ

**Q:** How do I know when it’s time to leave a movement space that feels off or toxic?

**A:** Your body is the most honest meter you have. Maybe it’s the tightness in your trapezius that shows up every Monday morning before class, the sinking in your gut when the instructor’s tone shifts, or the way your jaw clenches during group feedback. These aren’t just feelings; they are your nervous system sending Morse code. Toxicity in movement spaces often doesn’t announce itself with a neon sign. It creeps in through micro-aggressions, dismissive comments, or an atmosphere that stifles your breath and presence. When your psoas knots up before you even step into the room, when your diaphragm feels constricted as you anticipate the session, that’s your body whispering. Trust that whisper. Karla McLaren talks about anger as a messenger  -  here, your discomfort is the body’s way of waving a red flag. Leaving isn’t failure or weakness; it’s a fierce act of listening, of honoring the biology of your survival. This is not pathology. This is biology.

**Q:** What if I’m afraid to trust my body’s signals because I was taught anger or discomfort is ugly or wrong?

**A:** This fear is a familiar companion, especially for women raised in cultures that pathologize their fury. The masseter tightens, the neck stiffens, and suddenly that internal warning feels like shame. But the body remembers - Bessel van der Kolk reminds us that trauma is stored in the fascia, in the muscles that clench and the breath that shortens. When you feel that anger or discomfort, it’s not a moral failing; it’s a biological alert. Somatic guides like Resmaa Menakem teach that the body knows first and foremost, before the mind can censor or rationalize. Your jaw remembers what your mind tries to forget. Learning to trust these signals means gently naming them without judgment, observing the sensations in your levator scapulae or the subtle flutter in your diaphragm. It’s a practice, often slow and tender, but it’s the only way to reclaim your sovereignty. Herbs like [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20) or [Gaia Herbs PRO Sleep Formula](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004J32XIG?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be allies here, calming the nervous system so you can listen more clearly.

**Q:** How do I handle the grief of leaving a community I once loved, even if it was harmful?

**A:** Grief is the shadow that trails every ending. It settles in the shoulders, a heavy weight pressing down on the trapezius and the upper back, often most acute in the quiet hours just before dawn. Pema Chödrön’s teachings on tenderness toward suffering remind us that grief is not something to overcome quickly or shove away. It is a body story, a muscle memory of loss that needs acknowledgment. You might find yourself missing the laughter, the rhythm of the gatherings, the sense of belonging - even if those spaces no longer serve your wholeness. This is natural. Robert Augustus Masters describes grief as a process of unbinding, a slow unwinding of the psoas and the heart’s fascia. Don’t rush it. Allow yourself the full scent of that sadness - whether it smells like rain on cold earth or the faint musk of a worn scarf. Rituals with herbs like [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) for mood support or [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) for gentle uplift can provide a container for this sorrow. Remember, leaving is not erasing, but creating space for your body to learn new ways to move, breathe, and belong.
